stem: A race has \( [[n]] \) runners, and 1st, 2nd, ..., and \( [[r]] \)-th place medals will each be awarded to a DIFFERENT runner. In how many different ways can these \( [[r]] \) medals be awarded?
solution: Since the medals are distinguishable (order matters), the count is the permutation \( {}_{[[n]]}P_{[[r]]} = \dfrac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[r]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the medals as indistinguishable, dividing out the ordering that actually matters here.
This allows repeats (the same runner winning more than one medal), which isn't possible.
This forgets to divide out the unassigned runners' orderings.
This only accounts for arranging the medals themselves, ignoring the choice of runners.
This adjusts the correct count by an unrelated amount.
This adjusts the correct count by an unrelated amount.

stem: In how many different ways can \( [[n]] \) distinct books be arranged in a row on a shelf?
solution: Arranging \( n \) distinct objects in a row gives \( n! = [[n]]! = [[answer]] \) orderings.
distractor_rationales: This uses the circular-arrangement formula \( (n-1)! \) instead of the linear-arrangement formula \( n! \).
This treats each position as having \( n \) independent free choices with repetition, which overcounts.
This doubles the correct number of arrangements.
This only accounts for choosing the first two positions.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: A row of \( [[total]] \) flags is made from \( [[n1]] \) identical red flags, \( [[n2]] \) identical blue flags, and \( [[n3]] \) identical green flags. How many distinct arrangements of the row are possible?
solution: Since the flags of each color are identical, divide out the internal orderings within each color: \( \dfrac{[[total]]!}{[[n1]]!\,[[n2]]!\,[[n3]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats all the flags as distinguishable, forgetting to divide out the repeats.
This forgets to divide out the repeats among the green flags.
This computes only the number of internal orderings within each color, not the arrangements of the row.
This only accounts for placing the red flags among all positions, ignoring the blue/green split.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: In how many distinct ways can \( [[n]] \) people be seated around a circular table, if seatings that are rotations of each other are considered the same?
solution: Fixing one person's seat to account for rotational symmetry, the rest can be arranged in \( (n-1)! = ([[n]]-1)! = [[answer]] \) ways.
distractor_rationales: This uses the LINEAR arrangement formula \( n! \), forgetting that rotations are considered identical here.
This divides by \( n^2 \) instead of fixing one seat properly.
This fixes two seats instead of just one, over-correcting for the symmetry.
This multiplies back by \( n \), undoing the rotational-symmetry correction.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: A menu offers \( [[n]] \) different toppings. In how many ways can you choose \( [[k]] \) of them for a pizza, where the order in which you pick them does not matter?
solution: Since order does not matter, the count is the combination \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of ORDERED selections \( {}_{[[n]]}P_{[[k]]} \); dividing by \( k! \) is needed since order doesn't matter here.
This allows a topping to be picked more than once, which isn't possible.
This uses one fewer topping in the pool.
This chooses one fewer topping than asked.
This adjusts the correct count by the number chosen for no valid reason.
This adjusts the correct count by the number chosen for no valid reason.

stem: In how many ways can \( [[n]] \) distinct people be arranged in a row if two particular people, Alice and Bob, must sit next to each other?
solution: Glue Alice and Bob together as one block, giving \( n-1 \) units to arrange in \( (n-1)! \) ways, then account for their 2 internal orders: \( ([[n]]-1)!\cdot 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the adjacency requirement entirely, using the unrestricted arrangement count.
This forgets to multiply by the 2 internal orderings of Alice and Bob within their block.
This treats the group as if the block used up two full slots instead of merging into one.
This is HALF of the total unrestricted arrangements, which is not how this constraint works.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: In how many ways can \( [[n]] \) distinct people be arranged in a row if two particular people, Alice and Bob, must NOT sit next to each other?
solution: Take all arrangements and remove the ones where they DO sit together: \( n! - (n-1)!\cdot 2 = [[n]]! - ([[n]]-1)!\cdot 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the restriction entirely, using the unrestricted arrangement count.
This reports the count of arrangements where they DO sit together, the complement of what was asked.
This subtracts only one of the two internal orderings of the adjacent block.
This is an unrelated formula that does not correspond to this restriction.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
